questions.question: What is the general rule regarding formalities in Irish contract law?

options.A: All contracts must be in writing and signed to be enforceable
options.B: Contracts may be made in any form — oral, written, or by conduct — unless statute requires a particular form
options.C: Contracts must be witnessed by an independent third party
options.D: Commercial contracts must always be in writing; only consumer contracts may be oral

questions.explanation: The general rule in Irish contract law is that contracts may be made in any form — oral, written, or by conduct. Freedom of form is the default position. Statute, however, imposes formal requirements for certain categories of contract. Where statute requires writing or a deed, failure to comply renders the contract void or unenforceable.

questions.question: Under s51 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 (LCLRA 2009), which of the following must be in writing and signed?

options.A: All leases, regardless of duration
options.B: Contracts for the sale or other disposition of an interest in land
options.C: All contracts involving a price exceeding €10,000
options.D: Contracts for the supply of services involving land (e.g., building contracts)

questions.explanation: Section 51 of the LCLRA 2009 replaced s2 of the Statute of Frauds (Ireland) 1695 for contracts made on or after 1 December 2009. It requires that a contract for the sale or other disposition of an interest in land must be evidenced in writing signed by the party to be charged (or their authorised agent). An 'interest in land' includes a lease, a charge, a licence coupled with an interest, and an option to purchase.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT required to satisfy the writing requirement under s51 LCLRA 2009 for a contract for the sale of land?

options.A: The document must be signed by or on behalf of the party to be charged
options.B: All material terms must be evidenced in writing (though they need not all appear in one document)
options.C: The contract must be witnessed by an independent solicitor
options.D: The document must identify the parties, the subject matter, and the consideration

questions.explanation: Section 51 LCLRA 2009 requires that the contract (or note or memorandum thereof) be signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought — not that it be witnessed. The writing must identify the parties, the property, and the consideration (or contain all material terms). It need not be a formal document — a letter, email (arguably), or any document evidencing the agreement suffices, provided it is signed.

questions.question: What is the doctrine of part performance as it applies to contracts for the sale of land?

options.A: Where a party has partly paid the purchase price, they can always enforce the contract
options.B: In equity, where the parties have acted in a manner that unequivocally referable to the contract (acts of part performance), the court may enforce the contract despite non-compliance with the writing requirement
options.C: Part performance allows a party to recover damages for breach even if the contract is not enforceable
options.D: Part performance is only available where the other party has been enriched

questions.explanation: The doctrine of part performance in equity operates as an exception to the writing requirement. Where a party has performed acts that are unequivocally referable to the alleged contract (e.g., taking possession, making substantial improvements), equity may intervene to enforce the agreement despite the absence of written evidence. The leading Irish authority is Mackey v Jones [1959]. Part performance is preserved under s51(2) LCLRA 2009.

questions.question: What types of acts have been held to constitute 'part performance' for the purposes of equity's jurisdiction?

options.A: Merely negotiating the terms of the contract
options.B: Taking possession of the land with the vendor's consent and carrying out improvements, or paying a deposit in conjunction with other acts
options.C: Receiving a receipt from the vendor acknowledging the agreement
options.D: Signing a heads of terms document 'subject to contract'

questions.explanation: Acts of part performance must be unequivocally referable to the existence of a contract relating to the specific land. Taking possession with the vendor's consent and making improvements, or part payment combined with other consistent acts, have been accepted. Mere payment alone may not be sufficient (Maddison v Alderson [1883]). The acts must be explicable only on the hypothesis of a contract of the type alleged.

questions.question: The Statute of Frauds (Ireland) 1695 originally required contracts of guarantee to be evidenced in writing. Under the current law, where must a guarantee be evidenced?

options.A: A guarantee need not be in writing under Irish law
options.B: A guarantee must be evidenced in writing signed by the guarantor or their authorised agent
options.C: A guarantee must be executed as a deed witnessed by two persons
options.D: A guarantee must be registered with the Companies Registration Office

questions.explanation: The Statute of Frauds (Ireland) 1695 (as preserved for guarantees) requires that a contract of guarantee — a promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another — be evidenced in writing signed by the party to be charged or their agent. This remains in force. An oral guarantee is unenforceable. The writing need not be a formal document — a letter or note suffices provided it is signed and contains the material terms.

questions.question: What is the distinction between a guarantee and an indemnity for the purposes of the writing requirement?

options.A: Both a guarantee and an indemnity must be in writing to be enforceable
options.B: A guarantee is a secondary obligation (requiring writing) — the guarantor is only liable if the primary debtor defaults; an indemnity is a primary obligation and need not be in writing
options.C: An indemnity requires writing; a guarantee does not
options.D: The distinction is irrelevant — both are treated as guarantees for writing purposes

questions.explanation: A guarantee creates a secondary obligation — the guarantor promises to pay if the primary debtor fails to do so, and their liability is conditional on the default of the debtor. A guarantee must be evidenced in writing (Statute of Frauds). An indemnity, by contrast, is a primary obligation — the indemnifier agrees to keep the creditor harmless regardless of the principal debtor's default. An indemnity need not be in writing, as it falls outside the Statute.

questions.question: Which of the following Irish statutes requires certain hire-purchase agreements to be made in a particular form?

options.A: The Sale of Goods Act 1893
options.B: The Hire Purchase Acts 1946–1980 and the Consumer Credit Act 1995
options.C: The SGSSA 1980
options.D: The Companies Act 2014

questions.explanation: The Hire Purchase Acts 1946–1980 and the Consumer Credit Act 1995 impose formal requirements on hire-purchase and consumer credit agreements in Ireland. These agreements must be in writing, contain specified information, and be signed by both parties. Failure to comply with the statutory form requirements renders the agreement unenforceable by the creditor.

questions.question: Under the Electronic Commerce Act 2000 (Ireland), can electronic signatures satisfy writing requirements imposed by Irish law?

options.A: No — all electronic signatures are legally invalid as a matter of Irish law
options.B: Yes, in most cases — the Act provides that an electronic signature is as legally valid as a handwritten signature, subject to specific statutory exclusions
options.C: Only if the electronic signature is certified by an approved certification authority
options.D: Only for commercial contracts between business parties — consumer contracts still require wet-ink signatures

questions.explanation: The Electronic Commerce Act 2000 (Ireland) gives legal effect to electronic contracts and signatures. Under s9, an electronic signature satisfies any legal requirement for a signature in most cases. Excluded categories (e.g., wills, conveyances of land in traditional form) continue to require handwritten signatures. The Act implements the EU Electronic Commerce Directive and eIDAS Regulation principles in Irish law.

questions.question: A 'note or memorandum' of a contract for the sale of land satisfies s51 LCLRA 2009 only if it contains which of the following?

options.A: All terms of the contract, however minor
options.B: The material terms — sufficient to identify the parties, the property, and the price or other consideration
options.C: The date of the contract and the signatures of all solicitors involved
options.D: A declaration by both parties that they intend to be legally bound

questions.explanation: A note or memorandum satisfying s51 LCLRA 2009 must contain the material (essential) terms of the contract: identification of the parties, description of the property, and the consideration (price). Minor or incidental terms need not be recorded, but essential terms cannot be left to inference. Multiple documents may be combined (joinder of documents) to constitute the memorandum, provided they are connected.

questions.question: Can documents be 'joined' to constitute a sufficient memorandum for the purposes of s51 LCLRA 2009?

options.A: No — all terms must appear in a single signed document
options.B: Yes — several documents may be read together if they are connected, and at least one is signed by the party to be charged
options.C: Yes, but all documents must be signed by both parties
options.D: Only if the documents are physically attached to each other

questions.explanation: Several documents may be joined together to form a sufficient note or memorandum, provided they are connected (by reference or as a matter of physical attachment at the time of signing) and the document signed by the party to be charged expressly or impliedly refers to the other documents. This 'joinder of documents' principle allows a signed covering letter to incorporate the terms of an unsigned contract annexed to it.

questions.question: What is a 'deed' in Irish property law, and when is it required?

options.A: A deed is any signed written document — it is required for all contracts
options.B: A deed is a formal written instrument signed, sealed (or expressed to be delivered as a deed), and delivered — required for certain transactions including conveyances of freehold land and certain long leases
options.C: A deed is required only for transactions involving the State or a public authority
options.D: A deed is required only in commercial contracts above a specified monetary threshold

questions.explanation: A deed is a formal legal instrument that was traditionally required to be signed, sealed, and delivered. Under the LCLRA 2009, s64, a deed must be signed by the executing party in the presence of a witness who attests the signature. Deeds are required for certain transactions: conveyances of freehold land, certain long leases (over 3 years), grants of easements, and powers of attorney, among others.

questions.question: The Family Home Protection Act 1976 (Ireland) requires which of the following?

options.A: All family home sales to be approved by the Circuit Court
options.B: The prior written consent of both spouses/civil partners before a conveyance of an interest in the family home is valid
options.C: A mandatory right of first refusal for children before a family home can be sold
options.D: Registration of all family homes with a national property register

questions.explanation: The Family Home Protection Act 1976 requires that a conveyance of an interest in the family home by one spouse is void unless the other spouse has given prior written consent. This consent must be obtained before (not after) the conveyance. The Act protects the non-owning spouse's right to occupy the family home and applies even where the conveyance is between the owning spouse and a third party purchaser.

questions.question: Under s51 LCLRA 2009, what is the consequence of a contract for the sale of land that does not comply with the writing requirement?

options.A: The contract is void ab initio
options.B: The contract is unenforceable (not void) — it can be relied on as evidence of the agreement but cannot be specifically enforced without compliance
options.C: The contract is voidable at the seller's option only
options.D: The contract becomes valid after one year regardless of compliance

questions.explanation: Under s51 LCLRA 2009 (and its predecessor, s2 of the Statute of Frauds 1695), a contract for the sale of land that is not evidenced in writing is unenforceable — not void. The contract exists in equity but cannot be specifically enforced unless the writing requirement is satisfied or the doctrine of part performance applies. The party in breach of the writing requirement can use the lack of writing as a procedural defence.

questions.question: In Mackey v Jones [1959], a purchaser of land had taken possession and made improvements without a written contract. O'Briain J held:

options.A: The contract was void for non-compliance with the Statute of Frauds
options.B: The purchaser's acts of taking possession and making improvements constituted part performance in equity, entitling her to specific performance
options.C: The improvements had been made without the vendor's knowledge and could not amount to part performance
options.D: The purchaser was entitled to damages only, not specific performance

questions.explanation: In Mackey v Jones [1959], O'Briain J held that the purchaser's acts — taking possession with the vendor's consent and making substantial improvements — constituted sufficient acts of part performance in equity. The acts were unequivocally referable to a contract for the purchase of the land. Equity intervened to enforce the oral agreement through specific performance, notwithstanding the absence of a written memorandum.

questions.question: Under which circumstances is a lease of land for 3 years or less NOT required to be by deed under the LCLRA 2009?

options.A: A short lease of no more than 3 years, taking effect in immediate possession and at a market rent, need not be by deed
options.B: All leases, regardless of duration, must be by deed
options.C: A short lease need not be by deed only if the rent is less than €5,000 per annum
options.D: A short lease of residential property need not be by deed; commercial short leases must be

questions.explanation: Under s64 LCLRA 2009 and the old law preserved therein, a short lease of 3 years or less that takes effect in immediate possession at the best rent reasonably obtainable does not require a deed. Such leases can be created informally. However, leases for more than 3 years must be created by deed, and a failure to execute a deed may result in the purported lease taking effect as a tenancy at will or periodic tenancy.

questions.question: What is the rule in relation to contract terms that are agreed after the formal contract is signed in a land transaction?

options.A: Post-contract oral variations are binding provided both parties agree
options.B: Subsequent oral variations of a written contract for land may be unenforceable for want of the writing required by s51 LCLRA 2009, unless equity intervenes
options.C: Post-contract variations are always valid as variations only require consideration, not writing
options.D: Subsequent variations must be by deed to be effective in land transactions

questions.explanation: A subsequent oral variation of a contract for the sale of land faces the same s51 LCLRA 2009 writing requirement as the original contract. An oral agreement to vary written terms of a land contract may be unenforceable for want of writing. However, equity may intervene through estoppel or part performance if the variation has been acted upon, preventing the party who agreed to the variation from relying on the written terms.

questions.question: The Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010 (Ireland) provides that:

options.A: Civil partners have the same rights as spouses under the Family Home Protection Act 1976
options.B: A cohabitant's interest in a shared home can be protected by a court order after 2 years of cohabitation
options.C: The shared home of civil partners is protected from disposal without the prior written consent of both civil partners
options.D: All of the above

questions.explanation: The Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010 extended Family Home Protection Act 1976 protections to civil partners (requiring prior written consent of both partners for disposal of the shared home). It also created a redress scheme for cohabitants, including property adjustment orders after a qualifying period of cohabitation. All three propositions are correct.

questions.question: What is the significance of an 'entire agreement' clause in a written contract?

options.A: It prevents the parties from ever making future amendments to the contract
options.B: It provides that the written document represents the complete and exclusive agreement between the parties, excluding reliance on prior oral or written representations and negotiations
options.C: It makes the contract automatically a deed
options.D: It prevents the courts from implying any terms into the contract

questions.explanation: An 'entire agreement' clause states that the written contract constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and that all prior negotiations, representations, and understandings are superseded. It is a contractual attempt to give effect to the parol evidence rule. Such clauses may prevent claims based on pre-contractual representations, but they do not necessarily prevent claims for misrepresentation (Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown [2000]).

questions.question: Under what circumstances might equity enforce an oral contract for the sale of land on the ground of proprietary estoppel (as distinct from part performance)?

options.A: Only where part performance is also present
options.B: Where the landowner made an assurance as to the claimant's interest in the land, the claimant relied on that assurance to their detriment, and it would be unconscionable to permit the landowner to resile from the assurance
options.C: Only where the land is of a particular use or value
options.D: Only where the claimant has paid the full purchase price

questions.explanation: Proprietary estoppel can give rise to an equity in land independently of the writing requirements, where: (1) the landowner made an assurance about an interest in the land; (2) the claimant reasonably relied on that assurance; and (3) the claimant suffered detriment in reliance. Where these elements are established, it would be unconscionable to allow the landowner to resile, and the court fashions an appropriate remedy (Inwards v Baker [1965]; Gillett v Holt [2001]).
